interactions.summary: Jiang introduces Hannibal as the military genius who sees Rome as an inevitable expansionist threat, then contrasts Hannibal's strategic imagination with Carthage's merchant council and Rome's blunt war-machine response.
He narrates Cannae as Hannibal's supreme tactical trap and then makes the real puzzle Roman refusal: after the worst disaster imaginable, the Senate chooses more war instead of surrender.

speaker_notes.note: Continuous Jiang narration; quoted speech from Hannibal's envoys and the Senate is dramatized by Jiang, not a separate recorded speaker.

claims.claim: Jiang says Hannibal understood Rome as an expansionist military power that would eventually come for Carthage even during formal peace.
Carthage is modeled as a merchant republic whose wealthiest citizens dislike war unless it is profitable, so Hannibal has to buy political space for his campaign.
Hannibal's crossing of the Alps is framed as an almost impossible act that brings him to Rome's doorstep and lets him rally Rome's enemies inside Italy.
Rome's response to Hannibal is described as brutal and direct: after repeated defeats, it builds an 80,000-man army to throw at Hannibal and crush him.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical model of Roman military style

claims.claim: At Cannae, Hannibal uses geography, a concave line, cavalry superiority, and double envelopment to trap the Roman army in a circle.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical explanation of the Battle of Cannae

claims.claim: Jiang presents Cannae as a disaster that killed nearly 70,000 Roman soldiers, cost Rome 20 percent of its adult male population, and killed a third of the Senate.
The Senate's refusal to negotiate after Cannae is presented as the unique Roman act that Hannibal cannot understand and that allows Rome to win fifteen years later.
claims.temporal_scope: Historical interpretation of the Second Punic War

signature_moments.moment: Hannibal sees peace as temporary because Rome will not stop expanding.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Rome will not stop until it has conquered the world
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This casts Hannibal not as reckless but as the one Carthaginian who recognizes Rome's nature.

signature_moments.moment: Carthage's political obstacle is reduced to the merchant maxim that war is bad for business.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase condenses the later contrast between Carthage's profit logic and Rome's sacrificial logic.

signature_moments.moment: Rome's tactical personality is named as the opposite of brilliance.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The Romans are not creative. They are just brutal, bold, and direct.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It defines the Roman way of war against Hannibal's genius.

signature_moments.moment: Hannibal's line flips inside out and turns Roman confidence into a prison.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the concave of Hannibal's army turns upside down
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The visual makes Cannae memorable as a reversal of Roman force into Roman enclosure.

signature_moments.moment: After hopeless defeat, the Senate answers Hannibal with more war.
signature_moments.source_phrase: It's bleak. It's hopeless. But there are Romans. Romans do not surrender.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the central Roman reversal: the disaster that should end the war reveals the identity that wins it.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's name for the wealthy Carthaginian ruling body that supports Hannibal only when his campaigns are profitable.
The 216 BCE battle Jiang treats as Hannibal's great tactical victory and Rome's greatest test.
A tactic in which Hannibal's army surrounds the Roman force and attacks from the rear and sides.
The Senate's post-Cannae decision to continue war despite catastrophic losses.

uncertainty_notes.note: Transcript renders a Carthaginian political element as 'Arlacher'; context likely means oligarchy, but the pass does not correct the transcript.
